content.text: Every AWS Availability Zone has two identifiers: a
) that is unique within your account, and an
) that is a stable, cross-account physical identifier. The critical difference:
the same AZ name in two different accounts does not refer to the same physical location.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing that us-east-1a maps to different physical AZs (AZ IDs) in different AWS accounts

content.text: When AWS created the AZ naming system, it deliberately randomized the mapping between logical names and physical locations across accounts. The reason was load balancing: if every account mapped
to the same physical AZ, that AZ would receive disproportionate traffic simply because many tutorials and examples default to the first AZ in a list.
By shuffling the mapping, AWS ensures that workloads are spread roughly evenly across all physical AZs. The tradeoff is that you cannot use AZ names to coordinate placement
will always point to the same physical AZ for the lifetime of your account. They are only unpredictable
, where the region code is an abbreviated form of the region name and the AZ index is a small integer:
use1-az1, use1-az2, use1-az3, use1-az4, use1-az5, use1-az6
Note that the AZ index numbers are not always sequential (e.g.,
) and do not imply any preference or ordering. They are identifiers, not ranks.
Looking Up Your AZ Name-to-ID Mapping
The AWS CLI, boto3, and the Console all expose the AZ-to-ID mapping for your account. This is the first command to run when coordinating with another team:

# List all AZs in the current region with both name and AZ ID
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,ZoneId:ZoneId,State:State}' \
  --output table

# Expected output (mapping differs per account):
# -------------------------------------------------------
# |         DescribeAvailabilityZones                   |
# +-------------+------------+-------------+
# | Name        | ZoneId     | State       |
# +-------------+------------+-------------+
# | us-east-1a  | use1-az4   | available   |
# | us-east-1b  | use1-az6   | available   |
# | us-east-1c  | use1-az1   | available   |
# | us-east-1d  | use1-az2   | available   |
# | us-east-1e  | use1-az3   | available   |
# | us-east-1f  | use1-az5   | available   |
# +-------------+------------+-------------+

# Cross-account: look up the AZ name IN ANOTHER ACCOUNT that corresponds
# to a given AZ ID (run this in the target account)
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --filters Name=zone-id,Values=use1-az4 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[0].ZoneName' \
  --output text
When AZ IDs Matter: Cross-Account Scenarios
AZ IDs become critical whenever resources from multiple accounts must be co-located or low-latency connected. The three most common scenarios are:
can be shared from an owner account to consumer accounts via AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM). The consumer account must launch instances into the same
as the reservation. Because AZ names differ across accounts, you must match by AZ ID, not by name:

# Owner account (111111111111): create a capacity reservation in use1-az4
CR_ID=$(aws ec2 create-capacity-reservation \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --instance-platform Linux/UNIX \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \   # maps to use1-az4 in this account
  --instance-count 20 \
  --instance-match-criteria targeted \
  --query 'CapacityReservation.CapacityReservationId' --output text)

# Share the reservation via RAM
aws ram create-resource-share \
  --name shared-capacity \
  --resource-arns "arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:111111111111:capacity-reservation/$CR_ID" \
  --principals 222222222222

# Consumer account (222222222222): find the local AZ name for use1-az4
LOCAL_AZ=$(aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --filters Name=zone-id,Values=use1-az4 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[0].ZoneName' --output text)
# In this account, use1-az4 might be us-east-1c, not us-east-1a

aws ec2 run-instances \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --subnet-id "$(aws ec2 describe-subnets \
    --filters Name=availabilityZone,Values=$LOCAL_AZ \
    --query 'Subnets[0].SubnetId' --output text)" \
  --capacity-reservation-specification \
    CapacityReservationTarget={CapacityReservationId="$CR_ID"}

When two accounts peer their VPCs to keep traffic off the public internet, latency-sensitive services want to communicate instance-to-instance within the same physical AZ. To achieve this, the teams must agree on AZ IDs, then each account creates subnets in the AZ
that maps to those IDs locally:

# Team A runs this in account 111111111111
echo "AZ mapping in account 111:"
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,Id:ZoneId}' --output table

# Team B runs this in account 222222222222
echo "AZ mapping in account 222:"
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,Id:ZoneId}' --output table

# Both teams agree to align on AZ IDs: use1-az4 and use1-az6
# Team A creates subnets in the AZ names that map to those IDs in their account
# Team B does the same in their account
# Result: peered instances in use1-az4 talk intra-AZ with no cross-AZ transfer fees
Spread placement groups and partition placement groups are account-scoped. If two accounts want instances on the same physical hardware rack (e.g., for an HPC workload), they need to use the same
when selecting subnets in each account. Without AZ ID coordination, instances may end up in different physical AZs despite both being labeled
AZ IDs in Infrastructure as Code
When writing Terraform or CloudFormation that must work identically across multiple accounts, use
that resolve the AZ name for the current account at plan/deploy time:

# CloudFormation: resolve AZ name via Fn::Select + AvailabilityZones
# CloudFormation does NOT expose AZ IDs natively — use a custom resource or
# hardcode the resolved AZ name per account in a Parameter

Parameters:
  AZForUse1AZ4:
    Type: AWS::EC2::AvailabilityZone::Name
    Description: >
      The AZ name in THIS account that maps to AZ ID use1-az4.
      Run: aws ec2 describe-availability-zones --filters Name=zone-id,Values=use1-az4
           --query 'AvailabilityZones[0].ZoneName' --output text

Resources:
  AppSubnet1:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Subnet
    Properties:
      VpcId: !Ref VPC
      CidrBlock: "10.0.10.0/24"
      AvailabilityZone: !Ref AZForUse1AZ4
      Tags:
        - Key: AZID
          Value: use1-az4

condition key works at the region level. For AZ-scoped access control (e.g., allowing capacity reservations only in specific physical AZs), use the stable AZ ID in a policy condition on resource tags. Tag your subnets and reservations with the AZ ID as a tag, then condition on the tag:
This policy allows instance launches only into subnets tagged with AZ IDs
those subnets carry in this particular account.
Discovering AZ IDs Programmatically at Runtime
Applications that run in multiple accounts (e.g., a shared service deployed via Service Catalog) should discover the local AZ name for a given AZ ID at startup rather than having it hardcoded:

import boto3
import os

def get_subnet_for_az_id(az_id: str, vpc_id: str) -> str | None:
    """
    Find the subnet in `vpc_id` that resides in the physical AZ identified by `az_id`.
    Works correctly regardless of what the AZ is named in this account.
    """
    ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')

    # Step 1: resolve AZ ID → local AZ name
    azs = ec2.describe_availability_zones(
        Filters=[{'Name': 'zone-id', 'Values': [az_id]}]
    )['AvailabilityZones']
    if not azs:
        return None
    az_name = azs[0]['ZoneName']

    # Step 2: find a subnet in that AZ within the target VPC
    subnets = ec2.describe_subnets(
        Filters=[
            {'Name': 'vpc-id', 'Values': [vpc_id]},
            {'Name': 'availabilityZone', 'Values': [az_name]},
        ]
    )['Subnets']
    return subnets[0]['SubnetId'] if subnets else None

# Usage: deploy an instance into the physical AZ use1-az4, regardless of account
VPC_ID = os.environ['VPC_ID']
subnet = get_subnet_for_az_id('use1-az4', VPC_ID)
if subnet:
    print(f'Launching into subnet {subnet} (physical AZ: use1-az4)')
else:
    print('No subnet found for use1-az4 in this VPC')
IMDSv2: Discovering AZ ID from Within an Instance
A running EC2 instance can discover its own AZ name via the Instance Metadata Service (IMDSv2). To determine the
from inside an instance without AWS credentials:

# IMDSv2 requires a token (unlike IMDSv1 which allows direct GET)
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X PUT "http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token" \
  -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600")

# Retrieve the AZ name
AZ_NAME=$(curl -s -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: $TOKEN" \
  http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/placement/availability-zone)

# Retrieve the AZ ID directly (available since 2020)
AZ_ID=$(curl -s -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: $TOKEN" \
  http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/placement/availability-zone-id)

echo "AZ name: $AZ_NAME"  # e.g. us-east-1a  (account-specific)
echo "AZ ID:   $AZ_ID"    # e.g. use1-az4    (stable across accounts)

metadata path is particularly useful in shared AMIs or multi-tenant SaaS services where the same image runs across many customer accounts — you can log the stable AZ ID for capacity planning without knowing the AZ name of each customer's account.
structure, each member account has its own independent AZ name mapping. There is no mechanism to force all accounts in an Org to use the same AZ-name-to-AZ-ID mapping. The correct approach is:
Define all cross-account infrastructure in terms of
Tag all AZ-scoped resources (subnets, capacity reservations, placement groups) with an
tag containing the stable AZ ID.
resource to resolve AZ IDs to names in each account at deploy time.
Enforce AZ ID tagging via an AWS Config rule that checks subnets for the

# AWS Config rule: flag subnets missing the AZID tag
aws configservice put-config-rule \
  --config-rule '{
    "ConfigRuleName": "subnet-has-azid-tag",
    "Description": "Subnets must have an AZID tag for cross-account coordination",
    "Scope": {"ComplianceResourceTypes": ["AWS::EC2::Subnet"]},
    "Source": {
      "Owner": "AWS",
      "SourceIdentifier": "REQUIRED_TAGS"
    },
    "InputParameters": "{"tag1Key":"AZID"}"
  }'

# Auto-tag newly created subnets with their AZ ID using EventBridge + Lambda
# (EventBridge rule: source=aws.ec2, detail-type=AWS API Call via CloudTrail,
#  detail.eventName=CreateSubnet)
# Lambda handler resolves the AZ ID and calls ec2.create_tags on the subnet
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Sharing AZ names across accounts in documentation
Team B creates resources in the wrong physical AZ
Always share AZ IDs (use1-az*) in cross-account runbooks
Hardcoding AZ name in Terraform for a module used in multiple accounts
Subnet ends up in different physical AZ per account
Use data.aws_availability_zone.zone_id to resolve at plan time
Using AZ names in RAM capacity reservation sharing instructions
Consumer launches in wrong AZ; reservation unused
Document the AZ ID; consumer resolves local name themselves
Not tagging subnets with AZ ID
No way to audit cross-account AZ alignment
Tag all subnets with AZID=<zone_id> at creation
Assuming AZ IDs are sequential (az1 < az2 < az3 in distance)
No ordering guarantee; IDs are identifiers only
Never assume geographic proximity from AZ ID number
Finding AZ IDs in the AWS Console
The AWS Management Console surfaces AZ IDs in several places. Knowing where to look saves a CLI round-trip:
: The instance detail panel shows both "Availability zone" (the name) and "Zone ID" (the AZ ID) in the "Host and placement" section.
: Each subnet row displays the AZ name and a "Zone ID" column (may need to be enabled via the column selector).
: The reservation detail page always shows the AZ ID so you can communicate it to consumers in other accounts without knowing their local AZ name.
: The subnet list includes a "Availability Zone ID" column alongside the standard "Availability Zone" name column.
Checking Instance Type Availability by AZ ID
Not all EC2 instance types are available in every AZ. Because Spot capacity and newer instance families roll out incrementally, it is common for
. Always verify before committing a subnet choice for a new instance type:

# Check which AZs offer a specific instance type (output includes AZ ID)
aws ec2 describe-instance-type-offerings \
  --location-type availability-zone \
  --filters Name=instance-type,Values=c7g.4xlarge \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'InstanceTypeOfferings[*].{AZName:Location,InstanceType:InstanceType}' \
  --output table

# Cross-reference with AZ IDs to get stable identifiers
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,Id:ZoneId}' \
  --output table

# Combine both: only show AZs that have c7g.4xlarge AND include the AZ ID
python3 - << 'PYEOF'
import boto3
ec2 = boto3.client('ec2', region_name='us-east-1')

# Build name->id map
az_map = {
    az['ZoneName']: az['ZoneId']
    for az in ec2.describe_availability_zones()['AvailabilityZones']
}

# Get AZs that offer c7g.4xlarge
offerings = ec2.describe_instance_type_offerings(
    LocationType='availability-zone',
    Filters=[{'Name': 'instance-type', 'Values': ['c7g.4xlarge']}],
)['InstanceTypeOfferings']

for o in offerings:
    az_name = o['Location']
    az_id = az_map.get(az_name, 'unknown')
    print(f'{az_name} ({az_id}): c7g.4xlarge available')
PYEOF
Local Zones and Wavelength Zones: Extended AZ IDs
Local Zones and Wavelength Zones also have AZ IDs, but their format differs from standard AZ IDs. Understanding the difference prevents accidentally treating them as standard AZs:
EC2, EBS, VPC only — no internet GW
Local Zone AZ IDs contain the city code (e.g.,
for Boston) between the region prefix and the AZ index. This makes them visually distinguishable from standard AZ IDs in IAM policies and tag-based filters.

# List all zone types in us-east-1, including Local Zones and Wavelength Zones
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --all-availability-zones \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,Id:ZoneId,Type:ZoneType,State:State}' \
  --output table

# Filter to only standard AZs (ZoneType = availability-zone)
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --filters Name=zone-type,Values=availability-zone \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{Name:ZoneName,Id:ZoneId}' \
  --output table
) are per-account aliases that AWS randomizes to spread load. They are stable within an account but differ between accounts.
) are stable, cross-account identifiers for the underlying physical AZ. Use them for all cross-account coordination.
to resolve the correct local AZ name for any account.
EC2 instances can read their AZ ID directly from IMDSv2 at
and enforce it via AWS Config to enable reliable cross-account AZ alignment auditing.
